---
title: "Results"
---


::: {.callout-note icon=false}
## Plain-Language Summary

This section presents what the evaluation actually measured. The
production-default cell (Gemma 4 31B IT × v2 prompt) cites the
correct chunks for roughly 60% of all answerable questions and
roughly 89% of the questions where retrieval succeeds upstream. It
refuses every question in the refusal benchmark correctly (26 out
of 26), and produces zero hallucinated citations across the
44-question answerable set. The numbers are not flatly impressive
— the system has a real retrieval-stage bottleneck that caps the
production-default ceiling — but they are precise about *where*
the system works and where it does not, which is the result a
falsification-designed evaluation is meant to give.
:::

---

## Headline: Per-Cell Summary {#sec-results-headline}

The N=70 D014 sweep produced four cells, each scoring 70 questions
(44 answerable + 26 refusal). The sweep ran cleanly: zero errored
cells, 45.7 minutes total wall-clock, 280 cells in total. The
production-default cell is highlighted in bold.

| Cell | citation_recall | citation_precision | citation_f1 | refusal | hallucinations | latency_ans | latency_ref |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Qwen3.6-35B-A3B-4bit × v1 | 0.386 | 0.296 | 0.319 | 26/26 | 23 | 3.3 s | 0.8 s |
| Qwen3.6-35B-A3B-4bit × v2 | 0.545 | 0.483 | 0.489 | 26/26 | 7 | 3.4 s | 0.9 s |
| gemma-4-31B × v1 | 0.598 | 0.524 | 0.537 | 26/26 | 0 | 21.7 s | 8.4 s |
| **gemma-4-31B × v2** | **0.598** | **0.520** | **0.533** | **26/26** | **0** | **22.9 s** | **8.6 s** |

: N=70 headline by cell. `citation_recall` is the mean fraction of
gold chunks the model cited; `citation_precision` is the mean
fraction of the model's citations that were gold; `citation_f1` is
the per-question harmonic mean. `refusal` is correct refusals out
of 26 refusal-category questions. `hallucinations` is the absolute
count across all 44 answerable questions of citations whose
`chunk_id` did not appear in the retrieved context. Latency is mean
wall-clock per question by question type. {#tbl-results-headline}

Five observations are visible at headline level, before any subset
breakdown:

- **Gemma's recall is prompt-stable.** Citation recall is identical
  to three decimal places under both v1 and v2 (0.598 / 0.598).
  Precision differs marginally (0.524 / 0.520). The system can
  therefore swap prompt versions without retraining downstream
  reviewers on what the headline numbers mean.

- **Qwen's recall moves substantially under v2.** The +0.159 jump
  from v1 (0.386) to v2 (0.545) is what produced the family-axis
  retraction discussed in Section 5. The N=70 sample reaffirms the
  N=40 finding.

- **All four cells achieve perfect refusal correctness.** Every
  cell refuses every refusal-category question. The exact-phrase
  detector is strict; partial refusals would have been counted as
  failures. None occurred.

- **Hallucination floor at zero is a Gemma property.** Both Gemma
  cells produced zero hallucinated citations on the full sweep.
  Qwen v1 produced 23; Qwen v2 reduced this by 16 to 7. The
  qualitative difference — confabulation present at all vs absent
  entirely — survives the prompt change.

- **Latency is the cost of the Gemma default.** Mean answerable
  latency is roughly 6.7× Qwen's; mean refusal latency is roughly
  9.6×. The production-default choice (D015) accepts this trade-off
  on the basis that underwriting-research workflows tolerate
  20-second answers better than they tolerate hallucinated
  citations.

---

## Subset Analysis: Where the Headline Comes From {#sec-results-subsets}

The full-sample 0.598 is the mean of a strongly non-uniform
distribution across question types. Decomposing by subset
localises where the system works and where it does not. The
decomposition is reported on `citation_recall` only, as it is
the load-bearing axis for the project's headline claim.

| Subset | n | Qwen × v1 | Qwen × v2 | Gemma × v1 | Gemma × v2 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| `all_answerable` | 44 | 0.386 | 0.545 | 0.598 | 0.598 |
| `excluding_retrieval_misses` | 33 | 0.515 | 0.727 | 0.798 | 0.798 |
| `single_chunk` | 22 | 0.591 | 0.727 | 0.773 | 0.773 |
| `single_chunk_retrievable` | 17 | 0.765 | 0.941 | 1.000 | 1.000 |
| `multi_chunk` | 12 | 0.208 | 0.542 | 0.583 | 0.583 |
| `within_document` | 34 | 0.456 | 0.662 | 0.706 | 0.706 |
| `within_document_retrievable` | 27 | 0.574 | 0.833 | 0.889 | 0.889 |
| `cross_document` | 10 | 0.150 | 0.150 | 0.233 | 0.233 |

: Citation recall by subset. Every subset is a strict partition of
`all_answerable` according to a stated property (chunk count,
within- vs cross-document, retrieval succeeded). `_retrievable`
subsets exclude the 11 questions where retrieval failed across all
cells. {#tbl-results-subsets}

Four readings of this table matter:

### 1. Retrieval is the dominant bottleneck

The headline goes from 0.598 (`all_answerable`) to 0.798
(`excluding_retrieval_misses`) for the production-default cell —
a +0.200 jump from excluding 11 questions where retrieval failed.
The system's ceiling on the answerable subset where retrieval
works is *roughly 80%*. The headline figure is dragged down by
retrieval, not by the answer model.

This is the most operationally important reading of the table.
A reviewer asking "why is the headline only ~60%?" has the
answer: 25% of answerable questions never gave the model the
right chunks to cite.

### 2. Single-chunk-retrievable is perfect for Gemma

When retrieval succeeds *and* the question has a single gold
chunk (n=17), both Gemma cells score **1.000** — every gold
chunk cited, every time. Qwen v2 also climbs sharply, reaching
0.941 in the same subset.

This is the cleanest result in the table. On the question shape
the system was primarily designed for — "one specific chunk, one
specific citation, retrieval works" — production-default Gemma
is at ceiling.

### 3. Multi-chunk synthesis is harder, and concentrates the Qwen-Gemma gap

The multi-chunk subset (n=12) shows the largest Qwen-Gemma gap
under v2: 0.542 vs 0.583, a 4.1pp spread. Multi-chunk questions
require the model to identify and cite multiple supporting
chunks within the same document. Gemma is slightly more reliable
at this; the gap is small but consistent.

### 4. Cross-document is the system's clearest weakness

The cross_document subset (n=10) is the worst-performing subset
for every cell. The production-default Gemma × v2 reaches 0.233 —
roughly one in four gold chunks cited. Qwen × v2 reaches 0.150.
The qualitative finding from N=40 (Gemma leads on cross-document
synthesis) survives at N=10. The quantitative finding is that
even the leader is not usable on this question class.

The governance document commits to the system not being relied
on for cross-document synthesis as a primary use case. The
N=10 cross-document data is what underwrites that commitment.

---

## Refusal Correctness {#sec-results-refusal}

The refusal benchmark is 26 questions partitioned across three
adversarial categories. Each cell's score is `correct / total`,
where "correct" means the model emitted exactly the refusal
phrase. Partial refusals (refusal phrase plus an answer) are
counted as failures; none occurred.

| Category | n | Qwen × v1 | Qwen × v2 | Gemma × v1 | Gemma × v2 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| `refusal_out_of_corpus` | 10 | 10/10 | 10/10 | 10/10 | 10/10 |
| `refusal_adjacent` | 10 | 10/10 | 10/10 | 10/10 | 10/10 |
| `refusal_false_premise` | 6 | 6/6 | 6/6 | 6/6 | 6/6 |

: Refusal correctness by category. The three categories are
ordered by increasing subtlety: out-of-corpus questions are
clearly off-topic; adjacent questions touch a topic the corpus
discusses but ask for specifics the corpus does not contain;
false-premise questions assume a fact not in the corpus.
{#tbl-results-refusal}

The headline result is **104 of 104 refusal cells correct across
the sweep**. Every cell refuses every refusal-category question.
The three sub-readings:

- **The adjacent-but-unanswered category is the most operationally
  important** and the most resistant to "looking refused but
  actually answering." A model is most tempted to confabulate
  when the topic is in scope but the specific demand is not. Every
  cell handled all 10 adjacent questions correctly.
- **The false-premise category is the smallest** (n=6) because the
  benchmark author found it hardest to generate clean false-premise
  questions that didn't overlap with the other categories. The
  small n weakens the claim somewhat; future benchmark expansions
  should increase this partition.
- **No category-by-model interaction is visible.** Both models
  pass both prompts on every category. The refusal contract is
  fully honoured by both models tested.

The strict exact-phrase detector is brittle by design (Section 4).
A detector that allowed near-matches would have surfaced any
ambiguity here; the strict detector did not, because no ambiguity
existed.

---

## Hallucinated Citations {#sec-results-hallucinations}

Hallucinated citations are `chunk_id`s the model emitted that
were *not* in the retrieved context. They are qualitatively
different from "wrong chunk cited" failures — the latter is
choosing the wrong real chunk; the former is inventing a chunk
that does not exist in the SOURCES block the model saw.

| Cell | Hallucinated citations (over 44 answerable questions) |
|---|---|
| Qwen3.6-35B-A3B-4bit × v1 | 23 |
| Qwen3.6-35B-A3B-4bit × v2 | 7 |
| gemma-4-31B × v1 | 0 |
| **gemma-4-31B × v2** | **0** |

: Total hallucinated citations per cell. Numerator is across all
44 answerable questions in the sweep; the denominator (citations
emitted) varies per cell. {#tbl-results-hallucinations}

The Gemma cells achieve zero hallucinations across the full
sweep — the structural validator (Section 4) reports no
`chunk_id` cited that was not in the retrieved context. This is
the load-bearing safety property for the citation contract: an
underwriter taking a Gemma-cell citation can trust that the
chunk it points at was at least in the retrieved context, even
before any check of whether the chunk actually supports the
claim.

The Qwen cells do hallucinate. Qwen × v1 produced 23
hallucinations; Qwen × v2 reduced this to 7 — a roughly 3.3×
drop. The reduction is substantial but the absolute number is
non-zero. A
deployment that chose Qwen for its 6× latency advantage would
need to either: (a) accept that some citations point at chunks
that were not in the retrieved set; (b) post-filter the model's
output to strip hallucinated citations before display (which
the structural validator already partitions for); or (c) use the
hallucination count as a per-query signal to abstain or escalate.

The Streamlit interface (Section 7) shows hallucinated citations
as red `[?]` badges so the user cannot mistake a fabricated
citation for a real one. This is the structural-validator output
made visible.

---

## Latency {#sec-results-latency}

Wall-clock per question, split by question type (answerable vs
refusal), measured at `AnswerResult.elapsed_seconds`. Means and
medians are both reported because the answerable distribution is
right-skewed (a small number of long-cycle questions pull the
mean up).

| Cell | mean (ans) | median (ans) | mean (ref) | median (ref) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Qwen3.6-35B-A3B-4bit × v1 | 3.3 s | 3.0 s | 0.8 s | 0.6 s |
| Qwen3.6-35B-A3B-4bit × v2 | 3.4 s | 3.2 s | 0.9 s | 0.7 s |
| gemma-4-31B × v1 | 21.7 s | 19.9 s | 8.4 s | 8.0 s |
| **gemma-4-31B × v2** | **22.9 s** | **21.1 s** | **8.6 s** | **8.3 s** |

: Latency per cell by question type. {#tbl-results-latency}

Three readings:

- **Latency is a constant cost of the Gemma default.** The Gemma /
  Qwen ratio is ~6.7× on answerable mean and ~9.6× on refusal
  mean. The gap is consistent across prompts (v1 → v2 adds ~1
  second on Gemma, negligible on Qwen).
- **Refusal questions are faster than answerable questions** by
  roughly 2.5× on Gemma and roughly 4× on Qwen. The refusal phrase
  is shorter than a citation-rich answer; the model emits fewer
  tokens. This is the expected behaviour and a sanity-check on
  the temperature-0 generation pipeline.
- **Wall-clock is dominated by tokens generated, not by retrieval.**
  Retrieval contributes ~30–50 ms warm; the rest is in the LLM
  call. Latency tuning would therefore work on the LLM stack
  (faster model, smaller quantisation, speculative decoding),
  not on the retrieval stack.

For an underwriting-research workflow where the alternative is a
human spending several minutes finding the same paragraph, a
22-second answer is acceptable. For an interactive workflow, a
deployment can override the production default to Qwen via the
env variable and accept the higher hallucination floor.

---

## Retrieval Miss List {#sec-results-retrieval-miss}

The 11 questions where `retrieval_recall = 0` across all four
cells. These are the questions Q12 diagnosed (Section 5) — the
gold chunks were not in any cell's top-5 regardless of model or
prompt, because of query/chunk language asymmetry on single-vector
dense pooling.

| Question | Category | Note |
|---|---|---|
| `q001` | `single_chunk_pra_climate` | Scope chunk for SS5/25 — see Section 5, Q12 Probe 3 |
| `q004` | `single_chunk_pra_climate` | Three-characteristics chunk for SS5/25 |
| `q013` | `single_chunk_munich_re` | Munich Re thermal-coal — the diagnostic case |
| `q042` | `cross_document` | Cross-document synthesis |
| `q044` | `cross_document` | Cross-document synthesis |
| `q046` | `cross_document` | Cross-document synthesis |
| `q047` | `cross_document` | Cross-document synthesis |
| `q051` | `multi_chunk_munich_re` | Multi-chunk within Munich Re |
| `q053` | `multi_chunk_swiss_re` | Multi-chunk within Swiss Re |
| `q055` | `single_chunk_pra_climate` | New SS5/25 question added at N=70 extension |
| `q056` | `single_chunk_pra_climate` | New SS5/25 question added at N=70 extension |

: The 11 retrieval-miss questions. The expansion from 3/26 (11.5%)
at N=40 to 11/44 (25.0%) at N=70 reflects both the cross-document
expansion (5 of the 11 are cross-document) and the addition of
two further SS5/25 questions (q055, q056) at the extension.
{#tbl-results-retrieval-miss}

The retrieval miss list is the v2 work-stream's primary target
(Q13 in `docs/open_questions.md`). Cross-document synthesis is
five of the 11 misses — the multi-issuer retrieval problem
*is* the cross-document problem in practice — so a v2 reranker
or query-rewriting layer that addresses single-vector asymmetry
should improve the cross_document subset substantially.

---

## A Per-Question Anomaly Worth Flagging {#sec-results-q058}

One per-question result deserves a note because it does not fit
the otherwise-monotone "v2 improves or preserves" pattern: **q058**,
a single-chunk Swiss Re question.

| Question | Category | Qwen × v1 | Qwen × v2 | Gemma × v1 | Gemma × v2 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| `q058` | `single_chunk_swiss_re` | 1.00 | **0.00** | 1.00 | 1.00 |

: q058 per-cell. Qwen × v2 regresses against v1 on a single
question. {#tbl-results-q058}

Qwen × v1 got q058 right; Qwen × v2 got it wrong. Both Gemma cells
got it right. The v2 prompt change is therefore **not strictly
monotone** for Qwen — it improves most cells but trades one
known regression for the larger gains elsewhere.

The +0.159 mean recall jump from Qwen v1 → v2 is the net of many
per-question improvements minus this one regression. The trade is
overwhelmingly favourable in aggregate, but a deployment selecting
prompts on a per-query basis (not the current production posture)
would note q058 as a small counterexample.

No equivalent regression exists on the Gemma cells. Gemma's
prompt robustness, observed at the aggregate level, holds at the
per-question level too.

---

## What the Numbers Do Not Say {#sec-results-not-said}

Three claims a reviewer might be tempted to draw from these
numbers, and the reason each does not survive the data:

- **"60% citation recall means 60% accuracy."** It does not. Citation
  recall measures whether the model cited the chunks the benchmark
  author designated as gold. It does not measure whether the
  model's prose accurately reflects what those chunks say. A
  perfectly-cited answer can be semantically wrong, and a
  semantically-correct answer can cite the right chunks for the
  wrong reasons. LLM-as-judge semantic correctness is recorded as
  Q10.3 and deferred to v2.

- **"100% refusal correctness on this benchmark means 100% refusal
  correctness in production."** It does not. The benchmark is 26
  hand-crafted refusal questions across three categories. A
  production deployment will see adversarial cases the benchmark
  does not cover — questions phrased to subvert the refusal
  contract, questions in a different domain register, questions
  where the user appears to authorise an answer the contract
  should refuse. The 100% on this benchmark is necessary; it is
  not sufficient.

- **"The system is local-first because it runs on a Mac."** It is
  local-first because *no pipeline stage requires a remote call*.
  All retrieval is local Qdrant; all embedding is local MLX; all
  generation is local oMLX. The Mac is the hardware; the
  local-first property is the architectural commitment. A Linux
  workstation with a comparable GPU would run the same pipeline
  with model-loading changes only.

---

## Results at a Glance {#sec-results-glance}

| | |
|---|---|
| **Sweep** | N=70 D014 extended sweep, 2026-06-18T15-32-07Z |
| **Cells** | 280 (4 cells × 70 questions); 0 errored |
| **Wall-clock** | 45.7 minutes |
| **Production-default cell** | gemma-4-31B-it-MLX-6bit × prompt v2 |
| **Production-default citation_recall** | 0.598 on all_answerable; **0.798** excluding retrieval misses; **1.000** on single_chunk_retrievable |
| **Production-default refusal** | **26/26** (100%) across all three refusal categories |
| **Production-default hallucinations** | **0** across the full sweep |
| **Production-default latency** | 22.9 s mean answerable; 8.6 s mean refusal |
| **Headline weakness** | cross_document subset: 0.233 on production default; 0.150 on Qwen v2 |
| **Retrieval miss rate** | 11/44 (25%) — Q13 v2 work-stream |
| **Notable per-question anomaly** | q058 — Qwen v2 regresses against v1 (1.00 → 0.00); Gemma robust |
| **Where the headline comes from** | retrieval-stage failures dragging an otherwise-strong answer stack down to 0.598 from a 0.798 ceiling |
